coredata.dc:title: Bioinspired honeycomb-like 3D architectures self-assembled from chitosan as dual-functional membrane for effective adsorption and detection of copper ion
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Lei
Wang, Chenyang
Wu, Wenrui
Li, Xiaowei
Ma, Shujuan
Li, Cong
Shen, Yehua
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: Metal ions released through industrial and human activities are difficult to degrade in the environment, and copper (II) ingestion and accumulation in the body would cause harm to human health and even disease. The environmentally friendly materials for simultaneous detection and removal of toxic ions are urgently required to be developed. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with excellent optical properties as crosslinking agent introduced into chitosan-based membranes with freeze casting technique. The as-synthesized fluorescent membranes (CS-CDs) could generate fluorescent response to copper (II) with adsorption capacity (12.48–49.97 mg/g). Based on the stable fluorescence signal of CS-CDs and fluorescence quenching with copper (II), a simple fluorescence spectrometry method for detection of copper (II) was established. The fluorescence intensity of CS-CD-4 decreased linearly with copper (II) concentrations ranging from 0 to 350 μmol/L. Moreover, the 3D regular honeycomb-like porous structure of CS-CDs facilitated the mass transfer and provided more effective sorption sites. The adsorption isotherm and kinetics were more suitable for the Langmuir model (R = 0.9927) and pseudo-second-order model (R = 0.9989), which indicated the adsorption were monolayer adsorption and chemisorption, respectively. As a result, the CS-CDs with dual-functionalities of detecting and adsorbing copper (II) were successfully fabricated, demonstrating the potential application prospect in environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ice templating
Copper adsorption
Fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and modification of chitosan-carbon dots membranes
Fluorescence detection of Cu (II) by CS-CDs in aqueous solution
Adsorption ability of membranes for metal ions
Determination of Cu (II) adsorption capacity of CS-CDs
Studies on the adsorption behaviors of CS-CDs
Fluorescence selective and sensitive detection for metal ions by CS-CD-4
Adsorption ability of CS-CDs for copper (II)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CS-CD composite membranes and their sensing mechanism for Cu (II).

HIM images of (a–c) top surface, (d–f) lateral view and (g–i) transection of pore microstructure of CS-CD-2-4. (j–i) Mathematical statistical results of pore area obtained by Image J software. The red arrows indicated the growth direction of ice fingers. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) ATR-FTIR spectra of carbon dots, chitosan, CS-CD-4 and CM. XPS spectra of (b–e) CS-CD-4 and (f, g) CS-CD-4-Cu2+.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum and (b) fluorescence intensity at 658 nm of CS-CD-4 with 17 kinds of metal ions. (c) Fluorescence emission spectrum containing different concentrations of Cu (II). (d) Linear response curve for detection of Cu (II).

(a) Cu (II) adsorption isotherms of CS-CD-1-4 and CM at room temperature. The fitting of isotherm adsorption with (b) Langmuir adsorption and (c) Freundlich models of CS-CD-4. (d) Adsorption curve, (e) pseudo-first-order and (f) pseudo-second-order kinetic plots for the adsorption of Cu (II) versus contact time using CS-CD-4.
Detailed composition for fabrication of CS-CD-1-4.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The density was obtained by measuring the mass and volume with a side length of 5 cm.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the reported materials for fluorescence detection of copper (II) ions.
Comparison of the reported materials for adsorption of copper (II) ions.

head.ce:title: Bioinspired honeycomb-like 3D architectures self-assembled from chitosan as dual-functional membrane for effective adsorption and detection of copper ion

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Separation Science for Analytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal ions released through industrial and human activities are difficult to degrade in the environment, and copper (II) ingestion and accumulation in the body would cause harm to human health and even disease. The environmentally friendly materials for simultaneous detection and removal of toxic ions are urgently required to be developed. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with excellent optical properties as crosslinking agent introduced into chitosan-based membranes with freeze casting technique. The as-synthesized fluorescent membranes (CS-CDs) could generate fluorescent response to copper (II) with adsorption capacity (12.48–49.97 mg/g). Based on the stable fluorescence signal of CS-CDs and fluorescence quenching with copper (II), a simple fluorescence spectrometry method for detection of copper (II) was established. The fluorescence intensity of CS-CD-4 decreased linearly with copper (II) concentrations ranging from 0 to 350 μmol/L. Moreover, the 3D regular honeycomb-like porous structure of CS-CDs facilitated the mass transfer and provided more effective sorption sites. The adsorption isotherm and kinetics were more suitable for the Langmuir model (R = 0.9927) and pseudo-second-order model (R = 0.9989), which indicated the adsorption were monolayer adsorption and chemisorption, respectively. As a result, the CS-CDs with dual-functionalities of detecting and adsorbing copper (II) were successfully fabricated, demonstrating the potential application prospect in environmental protection.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, a large amount of industrial waste water was discharged into the environment with the expansion of industrial activities [1]. Heavy metal pollution is spreading throughout the world and becomes one of the most serious pollution in water body at present [2]. Additionally, metal ions are difficult to degrade in the environment, which was a continuing risk to ecosystem and a significant threat to human health [3]. Copper is one of the concerned heavy metal ions, which was continuously released into environment through industrial and human activities including electronic waste disposal, ore mining and smelting, metal processing and gasoline processing [4]. Once copper (II) is ingested and accumulated in the body, resulting in harmful human health and even causing diseases, such as Wilson's disease, neurodegenerative changes and other diseases [5,6]. Therefore, sensitive detection and effective adsorption of heavy metals in the environment is essential to reduce pollution and prevent human diseases.

A variety of strategies have been developed for removing metal ions from polluted water, such as chemical precipitation, electrolytic method, solvent extraction, ion exchange and adsorption [7–9]. Among them, adsorption is considered as one of highly effective methods for the removal of heavy metals from aqueous solution [10]. Several kinds of adsorbents, such as nanoparticles, flat membranes and resins and so on, have been fabricated [11–13]. On the other hand, detection of heavy metal ions in the environment is also an important part of the control of heavy metal pollution. Among the numerous detection strategies, ultraviolet visible spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, electrochemical method and atomic absorption spectrometry are the most commonly explored for the detection of heavy metal ions [14]. But they are complicated, time-consuming and costly to operate. Therefore, various metal ion sensor platforms have attracted wide attention and been developed rapidly, such as fluorescent probes-based organic molecules, nanoparticles and carbon dots (CDs) [15–17]. In particular, CDs, as a new type of zero-dimensional material, has been synthesized and exhibited unique advantages such as excellent solubility, outstanding biocompatibility and photoluminescence [18–21]. Ramanan et al. prepared highly luminescent CDs, which were applied in fluorescence detection of Au3+ ions in aqueous media [22]. Liu et al. developed a simple and rapid method for detecting Hg2+ ions based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots [23]. Although CDs as a new kind of inorganic luminescent materials have attracted more and more attention, the problems such as difficult separation in solution and toxicity needed to be addressed [24]. Besides, most of the materials were only utilized for either adsorption or detection of heavy metal ions [25], it was meaningful to develop dual-functional materials for simultaneous adsorption and detection, which can effectively improve the application value and efficiency of materials [26,27].

Membrane technique has been extensively utilized for the removal of heavy metal ions from industrial wastewater and domestic sewage [28]. Membrane as adsorbent to eliminate metal ions in effluents is considered to be one of the most effective water remediation methods [29]. Chitosan is a kind of natural alkaline polysaccharide with abundant reserves that is highly attractive because of its unique properties, such as antibacterial, nontoxicity, biodegradability and biodegradability [30–32]. As a result, chitosan membrane exhibits great potential in water treatment as a promising filter owing to its exceptional characteristics, such as high porous structure with excellent pore interconnectivity and flexibility with reasonable strength [33–35]. Recently, our group fabricated covalent organic framework-loaded chitosan membranes with hierarchical porous structure, serving as adsorbent for fast adsorption of heavy metal ions (Cu2+ and Cr3+) [36]. Igberase et al. produced chitosan beads to enhance the adsorption properties for copper (II) ions [37]. Although those chitosan-based materials were only utilized for heavy metal adsorption, it inspired us to design and fabricate novel dual-functional chitosan membrane which can be simultaneously used for both detection and adsorption.

In order to achieve simultaneous detection and adsorption properties of chitosan membrane, a simple strategy was proposed to fabricate a chitosan-carbon dots membrane (CS-CD) with bioinspired honeycomb-like 3D architectures, which was based on the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of carbon point binding with metal ions to achieve the detection of metal ions and chitosan provided adsorption sites for interaction with heavy metal ions. The strategy was facilely realized with freeze casting technique with chitosan as substrate and CDs as crosslinking agent. Fluorescence quenching of different metal ions and the heavy metal adsorption ability of CS-CDs were thoroughly investigated.

Chitosan was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutaraldehyde (50 wt % in H2O), ammonium chloride and sodium diethyldithiocarbamate were gotten from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, Mo, USA). Acetic acid and ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 25%) were supplied by Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Standard solutions of metal ions (Zn2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+) were prepared with ultrapure water from respective metal salts of Zn(CH3COO)2, Al(NO3)3, Ba(NO3)2, CaCl2, Cd(NO3)3, CoCl2, Cr(NO3)3, CuCl2·2H2O, Fe(NO3)3, HgCl2, KCl, LiCl, Mg(NO3)2, MnCl2, NaNO3, Ni(NO3)2 and Pb(NO3)2, which were also from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and modification of chitosan-carbon dots membranes

ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method reported in the literature [38] with some modifications. Briefly, 15 mL of glutaraldehyde solution were dispersed uniformly in 30 mL of ethyl alcohol, and the resulting homogeneous solution was transferred to a 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless reactor. The sealed reactor was heated at 150 °C for 2 h and naturally cooled down to ambient temperature. After rotary evaporation at 45 °C for 4 h, a transparent and viscous solution was obtained. Furthermore, the as-prepared CD-containing solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before use.

The specific preparation process of chitosan-based membrane was shown in Fig. 1 , and the detailed compositions were listed in Table 1 . Taking CS-CD-1 as an example, its preparation procedures were following. First, chitosan was sonicated in 35 mL of 1% acetic acid solution and added CDs with a mass ratio of 10:1, which formed a suspension solution. The resulting mixture was carefully transferred into a copper plate as a mold, which was placed in liquid nitrogen to rapidly freeze. Finally, the mold was transferred to a vacuum freeze dryer for 24 h to remove the solvent, and a film formed and assigned as CS-CD. Similarly, a control chitosan membrane fabricated without adding CDs was marked as CM.

Helium ion microscope (HIM) images were collected using Orion NaniFab Scanning helium ion microscope (Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by JEM 2100 (JEOL, Beijing, China). Attenuated total reflection-Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) spectra were measured on Thermo Nicolet IS50 spectrometer (Nicolet, Wisconsin, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on ESCALAB 250XI XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The fluorescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Zeta potential of particles in dispersive system was measured by electrophoretic light scattering on Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, UK). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was measured in Simultaneous thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu (II) by CS-CDs in aqueous solution

ce:section.ce:para: Material with excellent and stable fluorescence was a prerequisite for fluorescence detection. For comparison, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs suspensions without metal ions (0.25 mg/mL) was measured at the same conditions. The deionized water was used to prepare CS-CD suspensions and 17 kinds of metal ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+) aqueous solutions, respectively. In order to investigate the fluorescence selectivity of CS-CDs, the above suspensions and metal ions solutions were mixed to prepare the samples, which contained metal ions (200 μmol/L) and materials (0.25 mg/mL). The fluorescence sensitivity was measured by preparing a series of Cu2+ solutions with different concentrations (0–350 μmol/L), while the concentration of CS-CDs in suspension solution kept at 0.25 mg/mL.

In a typical quantitative assay, an amount of different final concentrations of Cu2+ (0–350 μmol/L) was separately added to the suspension containing 0.25 mg/mL CS-CD-4. The fluorescence intensities at 658 nm were recorded after equilibration for 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption ability of membranes for metal ions
Determination of Cu (II) adsorption capacity of CS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A mother solution of copper ion (10 mmol/L) was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of CuCl2·2H2O in water for subsequent experiments. Sodium diethyldithiocarbamate aqueous solution (10 mg/mL) was prepared under dark conditions and marked as copper reagent. According to the method previously reported [36], a series of copper ion standard solutions were prepared at concentrations of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100 μmol/L including starch indicator, NH4Cl–NH3·H2O buffer solution (pH 9.25) and copper reagent. Finally, standard curve was obtained through measuring absorbance of these standard solutions at 452 nm.

Adsorption behavior of CS-CDs for metal ions was investigated, and adsorption experiments were conducted with the change of parameters, such as metal ion concentration and contact time. To perform this, a piece of CS-CDs (10 mg) was soaked and oscillated in 10 mL of copper solution under 25 °C for 12 h. The concentrations of metal ion varied from 0.5 to 5 mg/mL. In addition, the adsorption capacity of CS-CDs on copper was measured at different time within 0–240 min. Based on the color reaction of copper ions and copper reagents, the metal ion concentration upon each adsorption experiment was determined as described above, and isotherm and kinetic model analyses were then performed. The adsorption capacity at equilibrium (Q
e ) was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
Q e
=
V
(
c 0
−
c e
)
m
where V (L) is the solution volume; c
o and c
e (mg/L) are the initial and equilibrium metal ion concentrations in solution; m (g) is the mass of adsorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the adsorption behaviors of CS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption isotherms revealed the relationship between the adsorbent concentration and the adsorption capacity at constant temperature. Both Langmuir isotherm and Freundlich isotherm were the most classical adsorption isotherm models. Langmuir adsorption model is assumed that the binding capacity of each adsorption site is equal, without affecting by the occupation of adjacent sites. This hypothesis indicated that the adsorption model was monolayer and the adsorption energy was evenly distributed over the entire surface.

The Langmuir isotherm equation is given by the following:
(2)
c e
Q e
=
c e
Q max
+
1
K L
Q max
where Q max (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity; K
L (L/mg) is the Langmuir constant.

The Freundlich model is an empirical equation describing the adsorption behavior of adsorbents on uneven surfaces at active sites with heterogeneous energy. Equation for the model was given by the following:
(3)
ln
Q e
= ln
K f
+
1 n
ln
c e
where K
f (mg/g) is Freundlich constant and n is the adsorption intensity.

Kinetic adsorption experiment could reveal the change of adsorption capacity with time that is often described in terms of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics. The pseudo-first-order kinetic model reflected the relationship between the adsorption rate and number of unoccupied sites in which the rate of occupation of adsorption sites were proportional to the number of the unoccupied adsorption sites. In the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, the adsorption rate was connected with the square of the product of occupied and unoccupied sites and the number available adsorption sites at equilibrium was related to that of the occupied sites. The pseudo-first-order described by the equation was as follows:
(4)
ln
(
Q e
−
Q t
)
= ln
Q e
−
k 1
t
where Q
t (mg/g) is adsorption capacity at a specific time; k
1 is the rate constant of the pseudo-first-order and t (min) is adsorption time.

The pseudo-second-order described by the equation was as follows:
(5)
t
Q t
=
1
k 2
Q e 2
+
t
Q e
where k
2 is the rate constant of pseudo-second-order.

Chitosan is prepared by deacetylation of chitin, which widely exists in nature such as the shell of a crustacean including shrimp, crab or insect [39]. Abundant amino groups of chitosan not only make it easy to be functionalized by various functional groups, but also facilitate it to interact with metal ions. In order to broaden the application of chitosan, membrane [40,41], hydrogel [42,43], gel microspheres [37,44], nanoparticles [45,46] and other morphologies [47] based on chitosan have been developed. Most of them are only used to adsorb heavy metal ions but not to detect metal ions. Herein, a chitosan membrane that could adsorb and detect metal ions was designed, as shown in Fig. 1. Based on the adsorption ability of chitosan to metal ions, glutaraldehyde-based CDs were introduced to fabricate novel dual-functional membrane. The preparation conditions were carefully investigated, and the detail information was shown in Table 1. As shown in Fig. S2, the membranes of CM and four kinds of CS-CDs were prepared, and their color gradually changes from pure white to yellow.

TEM image (Fig. S1) showed spherical morphology of synthesized CDs with an average diameter of 2.3 nm. As shown in Figs. 2 and S3, these CS-CDs were a kind of 3D-architectured membranes with multiple channels. It could be observed from Figs. S3a and 2a-c that the upper surface of CS-CD-1-4 was flat and possessed a large amount of holes with sizes of >1 μm, which were related to the presence of bubbles on the surface. In the freeze casting process, the ice fingers grew up in the freezing direction, and the chitosan solution was pushed into the gap by the ice fingers. The ice fingers then disappeared and a large number of pores formed in CS-CDs by freeze-drying. As shown in Figs. S3b and 2d-f, the red arrows indicated the growth direction of ice fingers, and the channels in lateral view of CS-CD-1-4 were parallel to the freezing direction. On the other hand, honeycomb-like 3D structures could be clearly observed from the view of perpendicular to freezing direction, as shown in Fig. 2 g–i. The areas of these ultra-large pores were measured and mathematically counted by Image J software (an image-processing software developed by National Institutes of Health). Because of the soft texture of CS-CD-1 with free-standing lamellar architectures, it was difficult to prepare the sample for HIM and following accurate measurement of pore structure (Figs. S3c and d). Fig. 2g–i showed the pore structures of CS-CD-2-4, and the mathematical statistical results of pore area were shown in Fig. 2j-l. The pore area of CS-CD-2 was mainly 3000–4000 μm2 (Fig. 2g and j). Although part of pore area in CS-CD-3 were larger than 5000 μm2, but most of pore area were less than 1000 μm2 (Fig. 2h and k). However, the pores of less than 1000 μm2 were dominant in CS-CD-4 (Fig. 2i and l). These results indicated that smaller pores formed with an increase of CD amount, which was related to the increase of crosslinking degree. As shown in Fig. S4, the enthalpy change of chitosan powder was more remarkable than those of CS-CDs at 300 °C, meanwhile, with an increase of CD content, the enthalpy change of CS-CD-1, CS-CD-2 and CS-CD-3 became less obvious, indicating higher crosslinking degree of CS-CDs.

Additionally, the densities of CS-CDs were calculated from the mass and volume of the square CS-CDs with a side length of 5 cm, and three measurements were taken to acquire the average value. As shown in Table 1, average densities of CS-CDs between 21.47 and 24.03 mg/cm3 were obtained, indicating that all of these membranes were ultra-light.

In order to speculate the reaction mechanism, ATR-FTIR was employed to characterize chitosan, carbon dots, CM and CS-CD-4. Compared with the spectrum of chitosan, the absorption peaks at 1556 cm−1 (N–H bending) and 1405 cm−1 (C–N stretching) were significantly enhanced in the spectrum of CM, which was attributed to a large number of hydrogen bonds formed by self-assembling of chitosan. There were characteristic absorption peaks of –OH (at 3356 cm−1) and –NH (at 3288 cm−1) stretching vibration in the spectra of both chitosan and CM, but there was only a wide peak in the spectrum of CS-CD-4, indicating that the amino group in CS-CD-4 was consumed. On the other hand, the absorption peak at 1749 cm−1, assigned as CO stretching vibration from CDs, and a new peak at 1632 cm−1, assigned as CN stretching vibration, appeared in the spectrum of CS-CD-4, further indicating that the CDs were successfully integrated into CS-CD-4 through the condensation reaction of amino and aldehyde groups.

XPS was performed to investigate the surface chemical composition. The full spectra of CS-CD-4 and CM were shown in Fig. 3b–e and S5. As observed in Figs. 3b and S5a, characteristic peaks of C1s at 285 eV, N1s at 399 eV, O1s at 532 eV existed in CS-CD-4 and CM. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of CS-CD-4 (Fig. 3 c), the peaks at 287.4, 286.1 and 284.8 eV belonged to –CO, -C-O- and -C-C- group, respectively. In Fig. 3d, the peaks at 399.4 and 402.0 eV were assigned as -C-N- group and –CN- group in the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s of CS-CD-4, respectively. The peaks at 535.0 and 532.6 eV belonged to –CO and -C-O- groups, respectively, in the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s of CS-CD-4 (Fig. 3e). However, only a peak of -C-N- group (at 399.4 eV) was clearly observed in the XPS spectra of CM (Fig. S5c), and only a peak of -C-O- group (at 532.9 eV) emerged in Fig. S5d. The result suggested that CS-CD-4 formed by the reaction of aldehyde group in CDs with amino group in chitosan.

The XPS spectra of CS-CD-4 after adsorption of Cu2+ were further collected and shown in Fig. 3f–g and S6. The peak at 934 eV could be assigned as copper ion in the full spectrum of CS-CD-4-Cu2+ (Fig. 3f). Furthermore, the binding energies were 953.6 and 933.9 eV, which were assigned to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, respectively. And two satellite peaks at 943.9 and 963.3 eV were associated with Cu2+ and functional group of carbonyl and amino groups [36]. Moreover, high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of CS-CD-4-Cu2+ were shown in Fig. S6a. The peak at 287.8 eV was assigned as –CO, which dominated relatively lower proportion compared to CS-CD-4 in Fig. 3c. Only one peak at 532.6 eV (-C-O-) was significantly observed from high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s of CS-CD-4-Cu2+, while the peak at 535 eV belonged to –CO was undiscovered. The above analysis strongly illustrated that –CO group took critical part in the adsorption of Cu2+.

The fluorescence property of CS-CDs was measured at the excitation wavelength of 490 nm. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence signal of CS-CD-4 at 658 nm was significantly higher than that of CS-CD-1, while CM without CDs could not generate fluorescence. This suggested that the fluorescence signal was related to the addition of CDs. Obviously, CS-CD-4 exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence quantum yield was an important parameter for evaluation of materials ability to convert absorbed light into fluorescence [15]. Thus, fluorescence quantum yields of CS-CDs were further estimated according to a standard method [48]. The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of CS-CDs were 490 and 658 nm, respectively. Therefore, rhodamine 6G with similar excitation wavelength was selected as the standard reference. The absorbance of CS-CDs and rhodamine 6G were measured at the corresponding excitation wavelength. The fluorescence quantum yield could be calculated according to the following formula:
(6)
Q c
=
Q s
×
F c
A s
η c 2
F s
A c
η s 2
where Q represents fluorescence quantum yield, A represents absorbance, F represents fluorescence intensity integral area, η represents the refractive index of solvent, c and s represent CS-CDs and rhodamine 6G, respectively.

The fluorescence quantum yield of CS-CD-1 was only 2.4%, indicating that the material generated weak fluorescence after excitation. The estimated quantum yields of CS-CD-3 and -4 were 14.2% and 14.8%, respectively. It was concluded that CS-CD-3 and -4 with excellent fluorescence properties were more suitable for subsequent fluorescence detection of metal ions in aqueous solution than CS-CD-1.

In addition, the physical stabilities of materials in solution played an important role in the determination of stable fluorescence [53]. Typically, the absolute value of zeta potential of the suspension was higher than 20 mV, proving that the solute exhibited sufficient electrostatic repulsion to remain stable in solution [54]. Therefore, to know the flocculation rate (stability of the solution) of the suspension, the zeta potential of suspension solution containing CS-CD-3 or CS-CD-4 (1 mg/mL) was measured to be 28.7 and 31.1 mV, respectively, indicating that sufficient positive charge on the surface of CS-CD-3-4 offered their stability of dispersion. As a result, CS-CD-4 was finally selected as the fluorescent probe for following experiments, which possessed excellent fluorescence property and better stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selective and sensitive detection for metal ions by CS-CD-4
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CS-CD-4 with different metal ions was subsequently investigated. The fluorescence intensities of CS-CD-4 (0.25 mg/mL) in the presence of various metal ions (200 μmol/L) solutions were measured, and the result was shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The CS-CD-4 exhibited strong fluorescence emission in blank solution and 16 aqueous solutions containing metal ions of Li+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+, respectively, while the fluorescence intensity was remarkably decreased after adding Cu2+, indicating that the fluorescence quenching occurred, and the quenching degree reached 71.04%.

In order to further investigate the potential detection ability of CS-CD-4 for copper (II), relevant fluorescence quenching experiments were carried out under different Cu2+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of CS-CD-4 decreased gradually with an increase of Cu2+ concentration from 0 to 350 μmol/L. A linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the Cu2+ concentration could be depicted in Fig. 4d, where the linear correlation coefficient (R) reached 0.9941. Thus, the CS-CDs-based fluorescence detection method exhibited a wide linear range for quantitative analysis and selectively detection of Cu2+. As listed in Table 2 , several materials with fluorescence property were reported to selectively detect Cu2+ ion [50], while a few materials could simultaneously detect two or more kinds of metal ions. For example, carbon quantum dots-metal organic frameworks could detect Cu2+ and Fe3+ [6], and N,N-dimethylformamide-carbon nanosheets could detect Cr3+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions [52]. Obviously, in our case, CS-CDs exhibited highly selective ability to detect Cu2+.

The generation of fluorescence signal was related to n-π* transition of carbon dots in CS-CDs, because CM without carbon dots could not generate fluorescence signal with the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S6a, the peak at 287.8 eV belonged to –CO in CS-CD-4-Cu2+, whose binding energy was 287.4 eV for CS-CD-4 before adsorbing copper (II) in Fig. 3c. The increase of binding energy indicated that copper (II) chelated with carbonyl group of CS-CD-4, thus affecting the fluorescence emission of CS-CD-4. On the other hand, the electrons in the O atoms of in CS-CD-4 were transitioned from ground state to excited state when illuminated by excited light. Then red fluorescence was emitted, which was the released excess energy when the excited electrons returned to the ground state. However, the excited state electrons of O atom were transferred to the d orbital of copper (II) when the surface of CS-CD-4 was occupied by chemically chelating with copper (II), resulting in fluorescence quenching. This was attributed to photoinduced electron transfer process, which was a common mechanism encountered in fluorescent sensors for heavy metal ions [55,56]. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching of CS-CD-4 by copper (II) was attributed to the multisite coordination between copper (II) and the oxygen-containing groups in CS-CD-4 [51]. The fluorescence quenching degree of CS-CD-4 depending on the concentration of copper (II) was calculated by Stern-Volmer equation:
(7)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K
S V
c
where I
0 is the fluorescence intensity of CS-CD-4 without metal ions, I is the fluorescence intensity of CS-CD-4 combined with copper (II), c is the concentration of copper (II), and Ksv is the quenching constant of CS-CD-4-Cu2+. According to the Stern-Volmer equation, the Ksv of CS-CD-4-Cu2+ was calculated to be 1.05 × 103 L/mol, demonstrating perfect fluorescence response of CS-CD-4 to copper (II) in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption ability of CS-CDs for copper (II)
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to being a cost-effective fluorescent sensor, the chitosan membranes with honeycomb-like 3D architecture were also promising as a potential copper (II) adsorption material for environmental remediation. Dual-functional materials for simultaneous monitoring and removal of heavy metal-ion in water were prepared, such as functionalized mesoporous photonic crystal film for mercury (II) [1], fluorescent microspheres for Au (III) [57] and fluorescent wood for Cr (VI) [8]. Chitosan was explored as an excellent natural adsorbent in removing a wide range of contaminants due to the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups [40,45]. To evaluate the adsorption capacity of functional chitosan membranes for copper (II), the CS-CDs were further selected for adsorption tests.

The maximum adsorption capacity was the key index to evaluate adsorption performance of materials. In order to investigate the influence of CD content on adsorption performance, CM and CS-CD-1-4 were used to determine their maximum adsorption capacity of copper ions. In this experiment, 10 mg of membranes were immersed in the 10 mL of copper (II) solution with different concentration respectively and shaken at 25 °C for 12 h. The result was shown in Fig. 5 a. It could be intuitively observed from Fig. S8 that the color of these membranes after the adsorption of copper ions changed significantly, in which white CM became blue, and the color of CS-CD-1-4 also changed similarly after the adsorption of copper ions. The CM exhibited a maximum adsorption capacity of 59.20 mg/g, while the maximum adsorption capacities of CS-CD-1-4 were lower than that of CM, and gradually decreased to 49.97, 39.43, 29.83 and 12.48 mg/g, respectively. Obviously, the maximum adsorption capacities of CS-CDs were gradually decreased with an increase of the content of CDs.

As shown in the high resolution XPS spectra of N 1s of CS-CD-4-Cu2+ (Fig. S6b), CS-CD-4 (Fig. 3d) and CM (Fig. S5c), the bond of -C-N- changed obviously after the adsorption of copper, indicating that amino groups played a role in the adsorption of copper ions. Therefore, CM with the largest number of amino groups exhibited the largest adsorption capacity for copper ions, and the decrease of the adsorption capacity of CS-CD-1-4 was mainly caused by the reaction of some amino groups with aldehyde groups. These satellite peaks at 943.9 eV and 963.3 eV in Fig. 3g were also associated with carbonyl or amino groups. Amine groups have been found to be the most effective groups for removing contaminants from aqueous solutions [58]. Generally, nitrogen-containing adsorbents, such as amine, hydrazine, thioamide and imidazoline, not only chelate cationic metal ions, but also adsorb anions by electrostatic action [37]. The adsorption mechanism of CS-CDs to Cu2+ mainly depended on the electron exchange between the amino group and Cu2+. And the reduction of adsorption capacity of CS-CD-1-4 on copper ions was related to the decrease of adsorption sites provided by amino groups.

In order to investigate the influence of coexisting ions on the adsorption of copper ions, the adsorption capacity of CS-CD-4 in the solution with Cu2+, Na+, K+, Li+ and Ba2+ were further verified and shown in Fig. S9. When the coexisting ion (Na+, K+, Li+ and Ba2+) concentration was 0.5 mM, the adsorption capacity of CS-CD-4 to copper ions decreased by 1.26%, 2.69%, 1.16% and 2.59%, respectively. In the presence of high concentration (2.5 mM) of Ba2+, the adsorption capacity of copper ions was 11.94 mg/g that only decreased by 4.2%. The results indicated that CS-CD-4 could still adsorb copper ions under the interference of coexisting ions. As shown in Table 3 , the maximum adsorption capacities of black wattle tannin-immobilized nanocellulose [59], sulfonated polyvinyl chloride-derived hydrochar [60] and chitosan beads [37] were 46.14, 48.9 and 52.6 mg/g, respectively, which were comparable to CS-CD-1. Although a few adsorbents possessed higher adsorption capacity than CS-CDs, their preparation process and intricate molecular design were more complicated.

Adsorption isotherms and kinetics curves were often plotted to describe the interaction between copper (II) and adsorbents. Langmuir isotherm model and Freundlich isotherm model were two classical models to verify adsorption behavior. The adsorption behavior was further studied using CS-CD-4 as an example. Under the same conditions, the concentration at equilibrium of CS-CD-4 under different initial copper ion concentrations was measured, and the relationship between c
e and Q
e was explored to establish the Langmuir isotherm. Meanwhile the relationship between log c
e and log Q
e was explored to establish the Freundlich isotherm. Fig. 5b and c were the linear fitting plots of Langmuir isotherm model and Freundlich isotherm model. As shown in Table S1, the R value of Langmuir isotherm model was 0.9927, which was higher than that of 0.7418 of Freundlich isotherm model, reflecting that the adsorption of copper ions by CS-CD-4 was more consistent with Langmuir isotherm model. According to Langmuir hypothesis, the adsorption of copper (II) ions by CS-CD-4 was monolayer adsorption, and the adsorption sites were evenly distributed on the surface, which was consistent with other materials [13,61,62].

Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics were used to describe the kinetics of the adsorption of copper ions onto CS-CD-4 with a time-dependent adsorption experiment. Fig. 5d showed the adsorption capacity was 10.97 mg/g when the contact time was 15 min, and then tended to be stable after that. Finally, the adsorption capacity measured at 240 min was 12.27 mg/g. This result indicated that CS-CD-4 possessed the ability to rapidly adsorb copper ions in solution, indicating great potential in the field of rapid wastewater treatment. This rapid adsorption ability might be related to bioinspired honeycomb-like 3D architectures of CS-CD-4 with excellent pore interconnectivity. The linear fitting curves with pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model were shown in Fig. 5e and f, respectively. The pseudo-second-order kinetic model was better to describe time-dependent adsorption behavior than the pseudo-first-order kinetic. As shown in Table S2, linear correlation coefficient of pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R, 0.9989) was much higher than that of pseudo-first-order kinetic model (R, 0.5620). The Q
2cal value calculated with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was 12.17 mg/g, which was similar to the Q
e of 12.27 mg/g in Fig. 5d, confirming that the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was suitable for the observed adsorption behavior. The pseudo-first-order considered that the adsorption rate depended on diffusion rate, while the pseudo-second-order assumed the formation of chemical bonds between adsorbents and metal ions as a rate-limiting step [2]. Therefore, these results strongly suggested that the adsorption of single copper (II) onto CS-CDs via amino groups, possibly by chemical adsorption by electron sharing and exchange between the adsorbent and adsorbate [65,66]. Based on the time required to achieve adsorption equilibrium, CS-CDs exhibited better ability than hybrid carbon-mineral nanocomposites [63], polyaniline graft chitosan beads [37] (Table 3).

A facile and cost-effective strategy for fabrication of bioinspired honeycomb-like 3D architectures self-assembled from chitosan was developed via freeze-casting technique with ice template. The resulting ultra-light and green membranes were produced by self-assembly of chitosan and CDs, and constructed honeycomb-like porous structure with abundant active sites. CDs with excellent optical property were integrated into chitosan membrane to generate the red emission at 658 nm. More interestingly, the fluorescence emission of as-synthesized composite membranes (CS-CDs) could be selectively quenched by copper (II). Thus, the CS-CDs-based fluorescence detection method was constructed to selectively detect copper (II) with a wide linear range from 0 to 350 μmol/L. At the same time, the CS-CDs proved strong adsorption with the maximum adsorption capacity of 49.97 mg/g. Both adsorption isotherms and kinetics curves confirmed that the adsorption of copper (II) by CS-CD-4 was monolayer adsorption via chemical interaction. As a result, the dual-functional membranes based on chitosan self-assembly with adsorption and detection of copper (II) were successfully developed, which not only reasonably avoided the disposal of carbon dots, but also provided more possibilities for the application of chitosan materials. It was optimistically expected that environmentally friendly CS-CDs could provide a direction for the development of environmental pollution control and remediation using membranes.

Lei Pan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chenyang Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenrui Wu: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaowei Li: Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shujuan Ma: Supervision, Conceptualization. Cong Li: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Yehua Shen: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Junjie Ou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
